description: Pipeline tuning guidelines for optimizing Logstash throughput. Start with System Health to ensure adequate CPU and memory before adjusting pipeline settings. Suggestions are guidelines based on current metrics, not definitive values.

options.content:
### Tuning Workflow

All suggestions are **guidelines** based on current metrics — adjust incrementally and observe.

| System Health | Pipeline Suggestions | Action |
|---|---|---|
| All green | Differ from current | **Tune pipeline** — adjust workers, batch size, or delay |
| CPU or heap red | Already match current | **Scale hardware** — add CPU cores or increase heap |
| CPU or heap red | Also differ | **Tune pipeline first** (free), then reassess system health |
| All green | All match current | **No changes needed** — system is balanced |

panels.description:
JVM heap usage for the Logstash process.

• Green (< 60%): Healthy. Room to increase batch_size or add workers
• Yellow (60–80%): Normal operating range. GC runs more often
• Red (> 80%): GC pressure is high. Do NOT increase batch_size or workers without increasing -Xmx
• If heap is red and Worker Utilization is high, increase JVM heap before adding workers

Node-level metric — shared by all pipelines.
panels.title: JVM Heap

panels.description:
Current JVM heap allocation (-Xmx).

This is the maximum heap Logstash can use. Compare with JVM Heap % to see how full it is, and with Suggested Heap to see if you need more.

Node-level metric — shared by all pipelines.
panels.title: Total Heap

panels.description:
Guideline for JVM heap size (-Xmx).

• If current heap usage < 70%: no change needed, shows current -Xmx
• If ≥ 70%: scales heap so current usage would be 60% of new max
• Formula: heap_used_bytes / 0.60

After changing -Xmx, restart Logstash. Set -Xms = -Xmx for best GC performance.

Node-level metric — shared by all pipelines. This is a guideline, not a definitive value.
panels.title: Suggested Heap

panels.description:
Number of CPU cores on the Logstash host.

This is the physical constraint for pipeline workers. Workers beyond this count will compete for CPU time.

Node-level metric — shared by all pipelines.
panels.title: CPU Cores

panels.description:
Guideline for CPU cores based on actual system CPU utilization.

Formula: ceil(cores × system_cpu / 0.70)

• Result ≤ current cores: system has enough CPU
• Result > current cores: system needs more CPU capacity

Based on total system CPU (all processes), targeting 70% utilization. This is a guideline, not a definitive value.

Node-level metric — shared by all pipelines.
panels.title: Suggested Cores

panels.description:
Total system CPU utilization (all processes, all cores).

• Green (< 60%): CPU headroom exists. Safe to add workers
• Yellow (60–80%): Getting busy. Suggested Cores may exceed current
• Red (> 80%): CPU-bound. Suggested Cores will be higher than current

This is the same metric used by Suggested Cores (targeting 70%). When this is red and Suggested Cores > CPU Cores, scale hardware.

Node-level metric — shared by all pipelines.
panels.title: System CPU

panels.description:
Current pipeline.workers setting.

• Workers = parallel filter/output threads per pipeline
• Max useful value = CPU cores on this host
• If Worker Utilization is < 20%, reduce workers to free CPU
• If Worker Utilization is > 80% and CPU has headroom, increase workers
• More workers → more JVM heap pressure (each worker holds one batch in memory)

Select a single pipeline for accurate values. With multiple pipelines selected, values are averaged.
panels.title: Workers

panels.description:
Guideline for worker count targeting 70% utilization.

Formula: ceil(workers × utilization / 70)

• Result > current: workers are saturated, add more if CPU allows
• Result < current: workers are underutilized, reduce to free CPU
• Result = current: balanced, no change needed

Check System Health first — if Load/Core > 1.5 or CPU is red, address CPU before changing workers. These are guidelines, not definitive values.
panels.title: Suggested Workers

panels.description:
Current pipeline.batch_size setting (events per batch).

• Default: 125. Increasing to 250–500 can improve throughput if workers are underutilized
• Larger batches = more events per worker cycle = better ES bulk efficiency
• Trade-off: larger batches use more heap per worker (workers × batch_size × avg_event_size)
• If JVM Heap is > 75%, do NOT increase batch_size without increasing heap

Select a single pipeline for accurate values. With multiple pipelines selected, values are averaged.
panels.title: Batch Size

panels.description:
Guideline for batch size based on worker utilization.

• Utilization > 70%: double batch size (max 1000) — workers are busy, larger batches improve bulk efficiency
• Utilization < 20%: halve batch size (min 50) — workers are idle, smaller batches reduce memory waste
• 20–70%: no change, batch size is balanced

Check System Health first — if JVM Heap is red, do not increase batch size. These are guidelines, not definitive values.
panels.title: Suggested Batch Size

panels.description:
Current pipeline.batch_delay setting (ms to wait for a batch to fill).

• Default: 50ms. Controls how long a worker waits for batch_size events before processing a partial batch
• If Worker Capacity is low (< 30%) and input is bursty, increase batch_delay to fill batches more completely
• If latency matters, keep batch_delay low (25–50ms)
• High-throughput pipelines with steady input: batch_delay barely matters since batches fill instantly

Select a single pipeline for accurate values. With multiple pipelines selected, values are averaged.
panels.title: Batch Delay

panels.description:
Guideline for batch delay based on queue backpressure.

• Backpressure < 10%: increase to 100ms — queue is empty, longer wait fills batches better
• Backpressure > 50%: decrease to 5ms — queue is backed up, process batches faster
• 10–50%: no change, batch delay is balanced

These are guidelines, not definitive values.
panels.title: Suggested Batch Delay

panels.description: Current worker utilization per pipeline. Below 20% means workers are mostly idle (reduce workers or increase input). Above 70% means workers are busy (good). Above 90% means workers are saturated (add workers or optimize filters).
panels.title: Worker Utilization

panels.description:
Fraction of worker capacity in use: worker_concurrency / configured_workers.

• 0.0 = all workers idle. 1.0 = all workers busy 100% of the time
• Below 0.3: Pipeline is input-starved. Batches are arriving slowly. Consider increasing batch_delay to fill batches more, or reduce workers
• 0.3–0.7: Balanced. Workers have headroom for bursts
• Above 0.7: Workers are heavily loaded. If you need more throughput, add workers (if CPU allows) or optimize filters
panels.title: Worker Capacity

panels.description: Ratio of output throughput to input throughput. Values near 1.0 mean Logstash outputs events at the same rate it receives them. Values below 1.0 indicate a bottleneck between input and output (filter processing, output backpressure).
panels.title: ES Bulk Efficiency

panels.description: Input and output throughput (events per second) from Logstash flow metrics. A growing gap between input and output indicates filter processing or output backpressure is creating a bottleneck. Uses pre-computed flow rates that handle restarts gracefully.
panels.title: Pipeline Throughput

panels.description: Time spent per event waiting to enter the queue. Calculated as derivative(queue_push_duration.ms) / derivative(events.in). Rising values indicate queue contention — the queue is full and producers are blocking.
panels.title: Queue Pressure

panels.description: Rate of write thread pool rejections per ES node. Rejections happen when Elasticsearch's write queue is full, causing Logstash bulk requests to fail and retry. Any sustained rejection rate indicates the ES cluster can't keep up with Logstash output.
panels.title: Write Rejection Rate

panels.description: Average time per indexing operation on each ES node. Calculated as rate(index_time_ms) / rate(index_total). High values indicate disk I/O pressure or cluster contention that will slow Logstash's ES output plugin.
panels.title: Indexing Latency

list.description: Aggregation interval for time series panels
list.label: Interval
list.name: interval

title: LS-04 Pipeline Tuning
